title: Ransomware activity detected on file server
description: CrowdStrike Falcon EDR detected active ransomware encryption on file-server-prod (hostname: FS-PROD-01), identifying the process SvcHost64.exe (non-Microsoft binary) executing mass file rename operations targeting the \\FS-PROD-01\shared-ops share. Approximately 14,700 files had already been encrypted with a .crypt extension before the behavioral engine reached the kill threshold. The volume shadow copy service was also found disabled — vssadmin.exe had been called 7 minutes prior to the first encrypt event, indicating pre-staged attack preparation. Falcon isolated the server from the network within 800ms of the behavioral trigger.
detection_method: CrowdStrike Falcon EDR — Behavioral IOA (mass .crypt file rename + VSS deletion on file server; 800ms automated isolation)

timeline.event: CrowdStrike Falcon fired critical IOA on FS-PROD-01: mass .crypt file rename at 4,200 files/min with prior VSS deletion; 14,700 files encrypted before process kill; server automatically isolated from network

Incident Commander l.navarro@company.com declared P0 war room; endpoint-team, infra-team, and CISO notified; backup-system mount points immediately disconnected to prevent encryption of backup targets

Initial attack vector identified: compromised service account SVC_FILEOPS (last legitimate use: Oct 18) used to deploy SvcHost64.exe via PsExec from a lateral movement pivot point (WS-OPS-112, not yet isolated); WS-OPS-112 also isolated

Extent of encryption confirmed: 14,700 files in \shared-ops\finance-docs affected; backup-system confirmed clean (last clean backup: Oct 21 23:00Z, 1 hour prior to attack); restoration from backup initiated

FS-PROD-01 and WS-OPS-112 reimaged; 14,700 files restored from clean backup in 87 minutes; SVC_FILEOPS service account deleted; all service accounts audited for similar privilege levels; incident closed

resolution_summary: CrowdStrike Falcon isolated FS-PROD-01 and killed the ransomware process within 800ms, containing the blast radius to 14,700 files in the \shared-ops\finance-docs directory. Disconnecting backup-system mount points within 2 minutes prevented the encryption from reaching backup targets. All 14,700 encrypted files were restored from the Oct 21 23:00Z clean backup (87-minute restore time). Both FS-PROD-01 and the lateral movement pivot WS-OPS-112 were reimaged. A full forensic investigation identified the attack chain: phishing → WS-OPS-112 compromise → SVC_FILEOPS credential theft via LSASS → lateral movement to file server → ransomware deployment via PsExec.
root_cause_analysis: The attack originated with a phishing email 72 hours prior that compromised the workstation WS-OPS-112 belonging to an operations team member. The threat actor conducted reconnaissance for 70 hours before extracting the service account SVC_FILEOPS credential via LSASS dumping (blocked by Credential Guard on other systems but not on WS-OPS-112 which ran an older OS build). SVC_FILEOPS held Domain Admin-equivalent rights over file server mounts — a privilege level far beyond what was needed for its function (automated file archiving). The attacker used PsExec to deploy SvcHost64.exe on FS-PROD-01 under the SVC_FILEOPS identity. The VSS deletion and encryption execution were pre-staged as a scheduled task, meaning the attacker had left the initial access point before the ransomware triggered.
business_impact: Critical impact to endpoint systems affecting service delivery
remediation_actions: CrowdStrike Falcon killed SvcHost64.exe process on FS-PROD-01 and placed server in automatic network isolation — 800ms from behavioral trigger to isolation
Immediately unmounted backup-system share mappings from all production file servers to prevent encryption of backup targets (completed T+2min)
Isolated WS-OPS-112 (the lateral movement pivot) from the network via CrowdStrike RTR command
Confirmed backup-system last clean snapshot: 2025-10-21T23:00:00Z (1 hour prior to attack); initiated restore of 14,700 encrypted files from \shared-ops\finance-docs

Deleted SVC_FILEOPS Active Directory service account; audited all 34 service accounts for Domain Admin or file server mount privileges — found 2 additional over-privileged accounts, downscoped immediately
Reimaged FS-PROD-01 and WS-OPS-112 from clean baseline images; ran CrowdStrike forensic sweep on all 12 servers that WS-OPS-112 had authenticated against in the 72-hour dwell period
Submitted SvcHost64.exe sample (SHA256: 3d8c1f7a2e4b9607...) and IOCs to CrowdStrike Intelligence team; confirmed attribution to FIN7-affiliated affiliate
preventive_measures: Credential Guard enabled on all remaining workstations running Windows builds below 22H2 (WS-OPS-112 was running 21H1 without Credential Guard)
Service account privileged access model redesigned: all SVC_ accounts now scoped to least-privilege JITA (Just-In-Time Access) via CyberArk PAM
Backup-system share mounts changed to read-only from production file servers; write access requires explicit time-limited PAM checkout
CrowdStrike behavioral IOA threshold for file server mass file operations lowered: >1,000 file renames/min now triggers automatic isolation (previous: 5,000)
Quarterly ransomware backup restoration drills scheduled: IT team will perform live file restore from backup within 4-hour RTO target
security_controls_updated: CrowdStrike Falcon prevention policy for file servers updated: vssadmin shadow delete now triggers automatic process kill and host isolation without console approval
Active Directory tiering model implemented: service accounts restricted to Tier 1 (servers) or Tier 2 (workstations) — cross-tier authentication now blocked by PAW policy
PsExec and similar admin tools blocked via CrowdStrike application control on all non-IT-admin workstations
SIEM correlation rule added: alert on any service account authenticating to >3 different hostnames within 1 hour (lateral movement detection)
documentation_updated: Ransomware Incident Response Runbook updated: backup mount disconnection procedure added as step 2 (before forensic imaging) with explicit SLA of T+2min
Service Account Privilege Audit Procedure formalized: quarterly review of all SVC_ accounts with sign-off by IAM team lead
Business Continuity Plan updated: finance-docs restore procedure documented with 87-minute actual RTO benchmark from this incident
Threat Intelligence Bulletin TB-2025-051 published: FIN7-affiliate TTPs, LSASS extraction indicators, and SvcHost64.exe IOCs
lessons_learned: The 70-hour dwell time between initial phishing compromise and ransomware execution is the most critical finding — the threat actor had extensive time to conduct reconnaissance, steal credentials, and pre-stage the attack while remaining completely undetected. Credential Guard's absence on a single workstation (WS-OPS-112) running an older OS build was the pivotal control failure that enabled the entire attack chain. Service account privilege over-scoping (SVC_FILEOPS with Domain Admin-level file server access) meant that a single credential theft gave the attacker all the access needed. Backups saved the data; the restoration took 87 minutes against a 4-hour RTO target, but the financial and operational cost of the investigation, reimaging, and business disruption was $234K.
follow_up_tasks: EP-2380: Enforce Credential Guard on all Windows endpoints below 22H2 — identify 67 non-compliant endpoints and schedule forced OS upgrades — Owner: Endpoint, Due: 2025-11-15
EP-2381: Deploy CyberArk PAM for all service accounts; retire all SVC_ accounts with standing Domain Admin rights — Owner: IAM, Due: 2025-12-01
EP-2382: Implement honeypot file shares with decoy files to detect active encryption earlier in the kill chain — Owner: Detection Engineering, Due: 2025-12-15
EP-2383: Conduct 72-hour threat hunt on all systems that WS-OPS-112 authenticated against during the dwell period — Owner: Threat Hunt, Due: 2025-10-29
resolution: 14,700 encrypted files restored from clean backup; FS-PROD-01 and WS-OPS-112 reimaged; SVC_FILEOPS deleted; attack chain fully mapped and IOCs shared with CrowdStrike Intelligence
action_taken: Resolved by endpoint-team within 281 minutes; financial impact $234K from investigation, reimaging, and productivity loss
detection_gap: Email gateway did not quarantine the macro-enabled phishing attachment at initial delivery; Defender Safe Attachments sandbox was triggered but the LockBit dropper's anti-sandbox evasion techniques (environment fingerprinting, delayed payload extraction) prevented successful detonation during the analysis window
what_went_well: CrowdStrike Falcon behavioral detection halted LockBit after approximately 14,700 files were encrypted — less than 1% of the total file share content
Backup system was air-gapped and fully intact; complete restoration was accomplished with no data loss and no ransom payment consideration
Swift host isolation prevented LockBit from spreading beyond the initial compromised workstation to other finance segment hosts
what_went_wrong: Email gateway failed to quarantine the macro-enabled phishing attachment at initial delivery; the LockBit dropper was delivered before URL and attachment reputation was updated
Macro execution policy allowed the employee to enable macros on an external-sender document; no IT-enforced block existed for macro-enabled documents from external senders
File server had no ransomware-specific canary files or honeypot directories that would have triggered behavioral alerts at the first file modification
